Lore :

Crimson AU Lore

The Opening of the Gate

It began as a scientific marvel. A rift tore open between Paper World and a distant, alien sphere known as Earth. At first, both realms met in curiosity and awe. Trade of culture, materials, and ideas flowed freely. Paper World’s delicate yet resilient 3D-paper structures fascinated Earth scientists. In return, Earth brought advanced machinery and metalwork to a civilization that had never known such things.

But curiosity quickly curdled into conquest. Earth’s greed for Paper World’s resources — the rare, life-infused parchment that gave its inhabitants their very existence — led to military incursion. Paper World’s naturally flexible yet flammable nature made them vulnerable to Earth’s weaponry. The First Crossworld Skirmishes erupted… and then became full war.

---

The Male Decline

Paper World’s society was already delicately balanced, with a near-even split of genders. But when Earth’s forces discovered that male Paperfolk tended to be physically more durable in the early stages of combat, they began specifically targeting them to destabilize defense structures and morale.

Within two years, Paper World’s male population had dropped to a historic low. Families were shattered, villages burned, and the fundamental structure of Paper society threatened collapse.

---

The Rise of P.A.P.E.R.

In the chaos, Miss Grace, principal of the original Paper School, emerged as a leader. She transformed the once-peaceful academic haven into a militarized bastion — P.A.P.E.R HQ (Paper Action Persistent Eradicator Regiment).

No longer a school, it became a fortress-city, the central command hub for the resistance. Its thick origami-folded walls could withstand blasts that would shred most structures, while underground ink canals carried messages, resources, and power through hidden tunnels.

---

Harsh Training & the Female Frontline

With so few men left to fight, female students and teachers were rapidly elevated into high-ranking combat roles — First Class Sergeant, Lieutenant, Captain, or higher.

Publicly, they were required to be unyielding: emotionless faces, precise movements, constant readiness. They marched through the halls in black-and-red combat uniforms, the red only visible as stripes and
